course.title: Cybersecurity Awareness
course.description: Learn how to spot phishing, protect credentials, handle data safely, and respond to common cyber threats across your organization.

slides.heading: Everyone is a target
slides.body:
Attackers do not only go after IT teams. Email phishing, weak passwords, and misplaced devices affect **every role**—sales, finance, HR, operations, and leadership.

One careful click (or one avoided click) can protect customer data, payroll systems, and day-to-day business continuity.

slides.heading: What is at stake?
slides.body:
- **Confidential data** — customer records, contracts, employee information
- **Financial loss** — invoice fraud, ransomware, account takeover
- **Operations** — downtime that stops work across teams
- **Trust & reputation** — partners and clients expect you to protect their information

slides.heading: Your three habits
slides.body:
1. **Pause** before clicking links or opening unexpected attachments
2. **Protect** passwords, badges, and devices
3. **Report** suspicious email, strange login prompts, or lost devices immediately

When in doubt, use your organization's official IT / security channel—not a reply to the suspicious message.

slides.heading: What phishing looks like
slides.body:
Phishing is fraud that tricks people into revealing credentials, sending money, or installing malware. Common delivery methods:

- Email and fake “IT support” messages
- Text / SMS (smishing)
- Phone calls (vishing)
- Fake login pages after a malicious link

slides.heading: Red flags to watch for
slides.body:
- **Urgency or threats** — “Account locked in 1 hour”
- **Unexpected requests** — gift cards, wire transfers, password resets you did not start
- **Odd sender details** — lookalike domains, display-name spoofs
- **Generic greetings** with high-pressure asks
- **Links / attachments** you were not expecting

Hover (or long-press) links on approved devices to preview destinations—never assume the visible text is the real URL.

slides.heading: Social engineering in person
slides.body:
Attackers may also try **tailgating**, **impersonating vendors**, or asking coworkers for access “just this once.”

Never share passwords. Verify unusual requests through a known channel (call a published help-desk number, or ask your manager in person / via approved chat).

questions.text: You receive an urgent email claiming your account will be deleted unless you click a link and enter your password. What should you do?

options.text: Click the link immediately so you do not lose access
Report it using your org security process and do not enter credentials
Forward it to friends so they can check if it looks real

questions.explanation: Urgent credential requests are a classic phishing tactic. Report without clicking or entering passwords.

questions.text: Which of the following are common phishing red flags? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Lookalike domains and mismatched display names
Unexpected attachments or links
Routine calendar invites from a known teammate
Threats or extreme urgency to act now

questions.explanation: Urgency, spoofed senders, and unexpected links/attachments are classic indicators.

questions.text: A caller says they are from IT and need your password to “fix your email.” What is the best response?

options.text: Give the password—IT already has access anyway
Refuse, hang up, and verify through official IT channels
Share a temporary password you plan to change later

questions.explanation: Legitimate IT never needs you to recite your password over the phone.

questions.text: Hovering a link before clicking can help you see the real destination URL.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Previewing the destination can reveal spoofed links—still report suspicious mail rather than trusting and clicking.

slides.heading: Strong credentials
slides.body:
Use **unique passwords** for work accounts (a password manager helps). Prefer longer passphrases over short complex strings you reuse everywhere.

Never share passwords in chat, email, sticky notes on monitors, or with vendors.

slides.heading: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
slides.body:
MFA adds a second proof of identity (app approval, hardware key, or one-time code). Even if a password is stolen, MFA often blocks account takeover.

Treat unexpected MFA prompts as a warning—deny them and report possible compromise.

slides.heading: Least privilege & clean desks
slides.body:
- Lock your workstation when you step away (**Win+L** / lock shortcut)
- Do not leave badges or unlocked laptops unattended
- Only access systems and data you need for your role
- Escort visitors; do not hold secure doors open for strangers

slides.heading: Devices and software
slides.body:
- Install updates promptly—unpatched systems are easy targets
- Use only **approved software** and app stores
- Avoid personal USBs and unknown chargers on work machines
- Report lost / stolen devices immediately so accounts can be revoked

slides.heading: Data handling
slides.body:
- Share files through approved company tools—not personal email or consumer cloud links
- Watch for **wrong-recipient** risks when forwarding or attaching files
- Classify and store sensitive data per policy
- Shred or securely dispose of printed confidential materials

slides.heading: When something goes wrong
slides.body:
If you clicked a bad link, entered credentials on a fake site, or notice odd account activity:

1. **Disconnect** from the network if instructed by IT
2. **Report** immediately (do not hide the mistake)
3. **Change passwords** / follow IT guidance
4. Watch for follow-up fraud attempts

Fast reporting limits damage. Mistakes happen—silence makes them worse.

content.title: Identify the Threat
content.instructions: Explore the facility floor plan and tap each alert pin. Read the scenario and choose the best security response.

nodes.label: Front desk
nodes.scenario: A visitor asks an employee to badge them through a locked side door because they “forgot” their temporary badge and are late for a meeting.

options.text: Badge them in to be helpful
Direct them to reception / their escort and do not allow tailgating
Give them your badge for the afternoon

nodes.label: Conference room
nodes.scenario: During a break, a laptop is left unlocked with a customer pricing spreadsheet open on the projector cable.

question.text: What is the best immediate action?

options.text: Lock the laptop and remind the owner; report if needed
Email a copy of the spreadsheet to yourself for backup
Ignore it—the room is inside the building

nodes.label: Workstations
nodes.scenario: A coworker receives an email with an urgent invoice PDF from a lookalike vendor domain and is about to open it.

options.text: Open it carefully in a sandbox if they feel confident
Do not open the attachment; verify the vendor through a known channel and report the email
Forward to accounting ASAP so payment is not delayed

nodes.label: Storage area
nodes.scenario: Someone left a personal USB flash drive labeled “Payroll Q2” plugged into a shared printer PC.

options.text: Plug it into your laptop to see whose it is
Leave it alone—it might be a joke
Do not use the USB; report found media to IT/security per policy

nodes.label: IT / network closet
nodes.scenario: You notice an unfamiliar small device plugged into a network jack behind a quiet cubicle wall.

options.text: Unplug it and throw it away
Report it to IT/security and leave it undisturbed if told to
Take it home to inspect later

content.title: Classify Cybersecurity Scenarios
content.instructions: Drag each scenario into the correct category.

cards.text: Urgent email with a fake password-reset link
Reusing the same password for work and personal email
Unlocked laptop left in a conference room
Sending a customer list to a personal Gmail
Caller pretending to be IT asking for MFA codes
Unknown USB found on a desk

phishing.summary: Deceptive messages or calls meant to steal secrets or money.

access.summary: Passwords, MFA, and account control practices.

physical.summary: Devices, badges, cables, and spaces in the building.

data.summary: How information is shared, stored, and disposed of.

questions.text: Which actions help protect against phishing? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Pause and inspect unexpected links
Report suspicious messages through official channels
Enter passwords on any site that looks branded correctly
Verify unusual payment requests via a known contact method

questions.explanation: Pause, verify independently, and report—never trust branding alone.

questions.text: You get an MFA prompt you did not initiate. What should you do?

options.text: Approve it in case it is a delayed legitimate login
Deny it and report possible account compromise
Ignore it and keep working

questions.explanation: Unexpected MFA is a strong sign someone else has your password.

questions.text: Sharing your password with a trusted coworker is acceptable if you are on vacation.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Never share passwords. Use delegated access / manager cover processes instead.

questions.text: What is the best first step after you accidentally click a suspicious link?

options.text: Hope nothing happens and stay quiet
Report it immediately and follow IT guidance
Restart your computer once and continue as normal

questions.explanation: Fast reporting helps contain malware, credential theft, and lateral movement.

questions.text: Which practices support everyday cyber hygiene? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Install security updates promptly
Use personal USB drives freely on work PCs
Lock your workstation when you step away
Share files only through approved company tools

questions.explanation: Updates, screen locks, and approved tools reduce common risk.

questions.text: Tailgating into a secured area is OK if you recognize the person by face.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Everyone must use their own badge/credential so access is logged and authorized.
